title: Dynamic and Topological Aspects of Hamiltonian Floer Theory on Surfaces
speaker: Dustin Connery-Grigg
abstract: Given a Hamiltonian H∈C∞(S1×M), what is the relationship between the dynamics of the isotopy generated by H and the various Floer-theoretic invariants associated to H? In this talk I will discuss how — in the case where M is a closed surface —one can interpret certain spectral invariants in purely dynamical terms,in addition to interpreting certain parts of the Hamiltonian Floer complex in terms of particularly nice transverse foliations initially introduced by Le Calvez in his study of equivariant Brouwer theory and surface homeomorphisms.

proof_stages.label: Dynamical questions and the continuation-cylinder transfer principle
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates the problem of extracting dynamical meaning from Floer chains, spectral invariants, and Floer moduli spaces, and announces the guiding idea that index-one continuation cylinders on surfaces let one transfer information from understood systems to arbitrary Hamiltonian systems.

proof_stages.label: Le Calvez transverse foliations as the target picture
proof_stages.summary: The speaker reviews unlinked fixed-point sets, maximal isotopies, and homotopically transverse singular foliations for surface homeomorphisms, presenting them as the kind of conceptual dynamical structure Floer theory should recover.

proof_stages.label: Braid language and MERM orbit sets
proof_stages.summary: Pure braids, braid homotopy, trivial and unlinked braids are introduced, leading to the definition of orbit sets that are maximally unlinked relative to the Morse Conley-Zehnder index range.

proof_stages.label: Index-one cylinder topology and MERM mapping-torus foliations
proof_stages.summary: For a nondegenerate surface Hamiltonian and Floer-regular data, the projections of index-one Floer cylinders between orbits in a MERM set form disjoint annular foliations of the mapping torus, with positive transversality to the suspended Hamiltonian flow, and this rests on asymptotic braid control, positivity of intersections, linking monotonicity, and a constructive continuation-cylinder theory.

proof_stages.label: Surface Le Calvez-type foliations and sliding maps
proof_stages.summary: The mapping-torus foliation is sliced and slid along its circle leaves to produce a loop of diffeomorphisms and a surface foliation for which a modified isotopy is genuinely positively transverse, with additional Morse-type structure and a relation between Hamiltonian sliding loops and Hofer geodesics.

proof_stages.label: PSS-image spectral invariants
proof_stages.summary: Chain-level PSS maps are recalled and used to define spectral invariants by minimizing over Floer chains hit by PSS data, yielding invariants with most standard formal properties and a comparison problem with Oh-Schwarz spectral invariants.

proof_stages.label: Maximal positive index-one braids and the PSS fundamental class
proof_stages.summary: Positive capped braids and maximal positive index-one braids are defined, and the central characterization states that these are exactly the Floer chains representing the fundamental class that occur as images of suitable chain-level PSS maps, giving a dynamical/topological interpretation of the associated spectral invariants.

examples.name: Intersecting periodic orbits force entropy
examples.note: The theorem that transverse intersections of periodic orbits relative to a Le Calvez foliation imply positive topological entropy illustrates why such foliations are dynamically useful.
